title: useEffect dependency array must list every external value
content:
Every variable, prop, or state read inside the effect goes in the dep array. Including functions. ESLint plugin react-hooks catches the omission.

Why: a stale closure captures the old value; the effect runs with stale data. Hard-to-trace UI bugs result.

When: every useEffect. If the list grows uncomfortable, the component is doing too much — consider useReducer or extracting a custom hook.

title: useState lazy initializer for expensive default
content:
useState(() => expensiveDefault()) instead of useState(expensiveDefault()). The factory runs once on mount; the eager form runs on every render.

Why: a JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(...)) on every render is silent perf rot. Lazy init confines the cost to mount.

When: default value involves I/O (localStorage, sessionStorage) or non-trivial computation.

title: useMemo / useCallback only when measurably needed
content:
Don't wrap everything in useMemo. The memo bookkeeping has a cost; for cheap computations (string concat, simple object literal) the wrapper is more expensive than the recompute.

Why: cargo-culting hooks slows the app and obscures intent. React DevTools profiler shows when memoization actually helps; trust the profiler, not a rule of thumb.

When: passing a non-primitive prop into a memoized child, or recomputation involves a measurable cost (>0.1 ms on the target device).

title: useRef for DOM access and instance values, not state
content:
useRef returns a stable container { current }. Mutating .current does NOT trigger a re-render — that's the whole point. Use it for DOM nodes, timer IDs, and 'instance variables' that shouldn't drive UI.

Why: useState would re-render on every change; useRef keeps the value alive across renders without notifying React.

When: focus management (inputRef.current.focus()), timer IDs, mutable counters that don't drive UI.

title: React.lazy + Suspense for route-level code splitting
content:
const Page = React.lazy(() => import('./pages/Page')); wrap in <Suspense fallback={<Spinner/>}>. Vite handles the chunking automatically.

Why: shipping the whole app on first paint is wasteful. Per-route splits get TTI down 30-50% on cold loads. Suspense provides the loading state without ad-hoc isLoading flags.

When: every route in a multi-page SPA.

title: Error boundaries on every route
content:
Class component with componentDidCatch + getDerivedStateFromError. Wrap each lazy route. Hooks have no equivalent yet — error boundaries remain class-based.

Why: an uncaught render error in one route crashes the whole tree. Boundaries scope the blast radius and let you show a 'something went wrong' fallback without taking the user back to a blank screen.

When: every top-level route, plus around any third-party widget you don't fully trust.

title: Vite env vars must be prefixed VITE_
content:
import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL is exposed to the browser. Anything not prefixed is server-only and unavailable in the bundle.

Why: Vite enforces this at build time to prevent leaking server-only secrets (DB passwords, signing keys) into client JS. The prefix is the contract.

When: every env var that needs to reach the browser. Server-only secrets have no prefix and live in .env (server) or process.env on the server runtime.

title: Prefer controlled inputs with onChange over uncontrolled refs
content:
value={state} onChange={e => setState(e.target.value)} keeps the state in React. Uncontrolled (defaultValue + ref) is fine for simple forms but breaks down with validation, conditional rendering, or shared state.

Why: controlled inputs are the React idiom for form data — easier to validate, easier to test, easier to reset. Uncontrolled mode mixes DOM state and React state and the boundary moves around.

When: any form with validation, conditional logic, or test coverage requirements.

title: TypeScript strict mode from day one
content:
tsconfig.json compilerOptions: 'strict': true, 'noUncheckedIndexedAccess': true, 'exactOptionalPropertyTypes': true. Add the rules later and you face hundreds of preexisting violations.

Why: strict mode catches the bug classes that account for most production incidents — implicit any, missing null checks, unsafe array access.

When: every new TypeScript project. For migrations, enable strict per-file via // @ts-strict and convert incrementally.

title: @vitejs/plugin-react-swc over @vitejs/plugin-react
content:
SWC compiles 10-20x faster than Babel. plugin-react-swc is a drop-in for plugin-react in 95% of projects.

Why: dev server hot reload latency drops from ~500 ms to ~50 ms on a medium-sized app. Cold starts are seconds faster. Worth swapping unless you depend on a Babel-only plugin (rare).

When: any new Vite + React project. For existing projects, swap the plugin and run dev — most apps work without changes.

title: Tailwind utility-first over CSS-in-JS for app styling
content:
Tailwind generates atomic classes; the production CSS bundle ships only the classes used (purge step). No runtime cost. Theming via CSS variables.

Why: CSS-in-JS libraries (styled-components, emotion) have a runtime cost — recreating styles per render. Atomic CSS removes the runtime and reduces bundle size 30-50% on style-heavy apps.

When: new projects. Existing CSS-in-JS apps can migrate gradually if the cost shows in profiling.

title: TanStack Query (React Query) for server state, not Redux
content:
Server state has different semantics than client state — staleness, refetching, cache invalidation, optimistic updates. TanStack Query treats it as such with one hook (useQuery, useMutation).

Why: Redux for server state ends up reinventing TanStack Query badly — async middleware, cache invalidation, request dedup. TanStack Query gets it right out of the box.

When: any data fetching beyond a single call. Pair with Redux/Zustand for client state if needed; many apps don't need any client state library.

title: Never use index as the React key prop
content:
Trigger:
    {items.map((item, i) =>
  • {item.name}
    • )}
. Looks fine in static lists. Consequence: when items reorder/insert/delete, React reuses the wrong DOM nodes. Inputs lose focus mid-typing, animations freeze, child component state leaks across rows. Alternative: a stable id from the data. If the data has no id, generate one once (uuid) and store it. Index is acceptable ONLY for static, never-reordered lists.

title: Never call setState in render
content:
Trigger: const [n, setN] = useState(0); setN(n+1); somewhere in the render body.

Consequence: infinite re-render loop. React detects and bails with 'Maximum update depth exceeded.'

Alternative: derive the value from existing state without setting (const m = n + 1). If you need a side effect on a condition, use useEffect with the right deps. If you need an effect at mount, useEffect with [].

title: Never mutate state directly
content:
Trigger: items.push(newItem); setItems(items). The reference doesn't change, so React skips the re-render.

Consequence: 'why isn't my UI updating?' debugging session. The state is correct in DevTools but the view is stale.

Alternative: setItems([...items, newItem]) for arrays. setItems({...obj, key: value}) for objects. Or use immer (produce) if mutation reads cleaner — Immer freezes the draft and returns a new object.

title: Never forget cleanup in useEffect
content:
Trigger: useEffect with addEventListener, setInterval, or websocket.connect — but no return.

Consequence: handlers accumulate across renders and across mounts. Memory leak; for global event listeners, fired N times instead of once after N renders.

Alternative: return a cleanup function. useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(fn, 1000); return () => clearInterval(id); }, []). For listeners: removeEventListener with the same handler.

title: Never use componentDidMount in new code
content:
Trigger: copying old class-component patterns into a fresh codebase.

Consequence: technically works, but mixes patterns. Future maintainer can't tell if your project is class-based or hooks-based; tooling targets the wrong mode.

Alternative: useEffect(() => { ... }, []) for the equivalent of componentDidMount. Keep the codebase one-style; convert remaining class components incrementally if you're migrating.

title: Never load .env into the client without prefix
content:
Trigger: in Vite, import.meta.env.SECRET_KEY appears undefined; you 'fix' it by exposing all env vars.

Consequence: server-only secrets (DB password, signing keys) end up in the JavaScript bundle, visible in DevTools.

Alternative: respect the VITE_ prefix. Server-only secrets stay on the server runtime — Express, Vercel functions, edge workers. The client gets only what's prefixed.

title: Never trust user input in dangerouslySetInnerHTML
content:
Trigger: rendering rich text the user supplied: <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{__html: userContent}} />.

Consequence: stored XSS. The attacker injects <script> or onclick=. Steals tokens, performs actions on the user's behalf.

Alternative: render as text (React's default does this safely). For rich text, sanitize via DOMPurify or a strict allowlist. Never pass raw user input through dangerouslySetInnerHTML.

title: Never render lists without virtualization at scale
content:
Trigger: rendering 5000 rows in a <ul>. The browser tries to lay out 5000 nodes; scroll FPS drops to single digits.

Consequence: unusable UI, especially on mid-tier hardware. Users blame the app for being 'slow.'

Alternative: react-window or @tanstack/react-virtual. Renders only the visible window plus a buffer. Performance is constant relative to total list size.

title: Never store JWT in localStorage if XSS exists
content:
Trigger: storing access token in localStorage 'because it's easier than a cookie.'

Consequence: any XSS reads the token and exfiltrates it. Once leaked, the attacker is the user until expiry — and you can't revoke localStorage.

Alternative: HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict cookies for session tokens. The browser sends them automatically; XSS can't read them. CSRF mitigation via SameSite + double-submit token if needed.

title: Vite over webpack for new React projects
content:
Chose Vite. Alternatives considered: webpack (with babel-loader), Next.js (full framework), Parcel.

Reason: 10-100x faster dev server startup; hot reload feels instant; simpler config; ESM-native. Webpack is fine but configuration sprawl is real and dev DX has fallen behind. Next.js if SSR/RSC is needed; for SPAs, Vite is the better choice.

Reversible: yes; both build tools output static assets. Migration takes a day for a typical SPA.

title: TanStack Query over SWR for data fetching
content:
Chose TanStack Query. Alternatives considered: SWR, Apollo Client (GraphQL only), redux-toolkit-query.

Reason: bigger feature set (mutations, infinite queries, optimistic updates, devtools), broader community, framework-agnostic so the same hook idiom works in Vue/Solid. SWR is leaner and simpler — fine for read-mostly apps.

Reversible: yes; the hook surface is similar. Migration is mechanical for queries, more thought for mutations.

title: Bundle analyzer reveals 80% of perf wins in 10 minutes
content:
Run rollup-plugin-visualizer (vite-plugin-visualizer) on the production build. Inspect the treemap — usually 1-2 dependencies dominate (moment.js, lodash full import, big icon library). Tree-shake or replace.

Why: most React perf advice is generic. The actual fix is project-specific and visible in the treemap. Date-fns over moment.js, named imports from lodash, lucide-react with single-icon imports — these are 100s of KB at a time.

When: every production build that hits 1 MB+. Re-run quarterly as deps grow.

title: Pre-rendering catches more SEO bugs than testing
content:
vite-plugin-ssr or vite-plugin-prerender on key routes (homepage, pricing, docs). Build emits static HTML for indexable pages.

Why: client-rendered React loads, then hydrates, then renders. Crawlers (especially older ones) see the empty shell and bail. Pre-rendering eliminates that class of SEO miss completely.

When: marketing sites, docs sites, anything that needs to rank. Not needed for app dashboards behind login.

title: Component file co-location: TSX + CSS + test in one folder
content:
src/components/Button/{Button.tsx, Button.module.css, Button.test.tsx, index.ts}. The folder is the unit. Imports use the folder name.

Why: when a component changes, all its files change together. Co-location makes that obvious in PR diffs. Refactors (rename, delete, move) act on the whole unit.

When: any project with more than ~5 components. For tiny utilities, a flat file is fine.

title: Use CSS custom properties for theming, not React Context
content:
Define :root { --color-primary: #ABC; } and use var(--color-primary) in CSS. Theme switch sets the property at the root; every cascade element re-reads.

Why: zero React re-renders for theme changes. Tailwind theming via CSS variables works the same way. ContextAPI for theming triggers a full re-render of every consumer.

When: design system theming, dark/light mode, brand color overrides.

title: Form validation: schema-first with zod or yup
content:
Define a zod schema once. Reuse for: client-side validation (react-hook-form + zodResolver), API request body validation (server side), TypeScript types (z.infer).

Why: one schema, three sites of truth in sync. Hand-writing validation in three places drifts within a sprint.

When: any form with non-trivial validation. zod is currently the dominant choice; yup is fine but smaller community.

title: Never useEffect to derive state from props
content:
Trigger: const [doubled, setDoubled] = useState(value*2); useEffect(() => setDoubled(value*2), [value]);.

Consequence: extra render every prop change; doubled lags one render behind value; bugs around initial mount.

Alternative: derive directly. const doubled = value*2; (or useMemo if expensive). Only use state when the derived value can change for reasons OTHER than props/state.

title: Storybook doubles as the design-system docs
content:
Stories per variant. CSF format (Component Story Format). Use storybook-tester for visual regression.

Why: the design system is whatever the team uses. Stories are runnable docs that can't drift from code. New devs onboard against Storybook, not a Confluence page.

When: design system worth more than ~10 components. Below that, the maintenance overhead exceeds the value.
